title: Do Electrolytes Actually Help Hangovers?
metaDescription: Electrolytes help with hangover dehydration symptoms but won't cure everything. Here's what actually works and what doesn't for hangover recovery.
intro: You've seen the ads: electrolyte packets marketed as hangover cures. There's some truth to it, but it's not the full picture.

sections.heading: Why Alcohol Causes Hangovers
sections.body:
Hangovers are caused by multiple mechanisms, not just dehydration:

**1. Dehydration and electrolyte loss.** Alcohol is a diuretic -- it inhibits ADH (antidiuretic hormone), making you urinate more. You lose water, sodium, potassium, and magnesium.

**2. Acetaldehyde toxicity.** Your liver converts alcohol to acetaldehyde (a toxic compound) before converting it to harmless acetate. If you drink faster than your liver can process, acetaldehyde accumulates and causes nausea, headache, and general misery.

**3. Inflammation.** Alcohol triggers systemic inflammation. This is why you feel achy and brain-fogged.

**4. Poor sleep.** Alcohol disrupts REM sleep. Even if you sleep 8 hours, the quality is terrible.

**5. Blood sugar disruption.** Alcohol impairs gluconeogenesis, causing low blood sugar the next morning (shakiness, fatigue, irritability).

sections.heading: What Electrolytes Fix (And Don't Fix)
sections.body:
Electrolytes address **problem #1 only** -- dehydration and mineral loss. This helps with:
- Headache (partially -- dehydration headaches respond, inflammation headaches don't)
- Thirst and dry mouth
- Dizziness
- Fatigue (partially)

Electrolytes do NOT help with:
- Nausea (that's acetaldehyde)
- Brain fog (that's poor sleep + inflammation)
- Anxiety/irritability (that's neurotransmitter disruption)

So electrolytes address maybe 30-40% of hangover symptoms. Better than nothing, but not a cure.

sections.heading: A More Complete Recovery Stack
sections.body:
If you want to actually feel better:

**Before bed (after drinking):**
- Electrolytes (sodium, potassium, magnesium) with 16-20oz water
- NAC 600mg -- supports glutathione production for acetaldehyde processing. **Must take before or during drinking, not the morning after** (NAC taken after alcohol may worsen liver toxicity according to some animal studies)
- B-Complex -- Alcohol depletes B vitamins, especially B1 (thiamine)

**Morning after:**
- More electrolytes with water
- Ginger 250mg or ginger tea -- For nausea (a proven antiemetic)
- Omega-3 or turmeric -- For inflammation
- Easy-to-digest food with protein and carbs

**What doesn't work:**
- "Hair of the dog" (more alcohol) -- Just delays the hangover
- Coffee -- Further dehydrates you. Wait until you've rehydrated.
- Greasy food -- Helps before drinking (slows absorption), not after
- Activated charcoal -- Doesn't bind alcohol effectively

sections.heading: Prevention Beats Cure
sections.body:
The most effective "hangover supplement" is prevention:

- Eat a substantial meal before drinking
- Alternate every alcoholic drink with water
- Stick to clear liquors (fewer congeners -- the compounds that worsen hangovers)
- Take electrolytes before bed, not the next morning
- Set a drink limit and stick to it

No supplement stack can overcome 12 drinks. But for moderate drinking, electrolytes + B vitamins + NAC (before drinking) + ginger (after) is the most evidence-based recovery protocol.

Check supplement interactions with our [Supplement Checker](/).

title: Lion's Mane: Benefits, Dosage, and Side Effects
metaDescription: Lion's mane mushroom stimulates nerve growth factor and may improve memory and focus. Here's the complete guide to benefits, dosing, and what to expect.
intro: Lion's mane is the most interesting nootropic mushroom because it does something no other legal supplement can do: stimulate nerve growth factor (NGF) production. Here's what that means for you.

sections.heading: How It Works
sections.body:
Lion's mane (Hericium erinaceus) contains two unique compounds:

**Hericenones** (found in the fruiting body) -- Cross the blood-brain barrier and stimulate NGF production in the brain.

**Erinacines** (found in the mycelium) -- Also stimulate NGF, and some research suggests they're more potent than hericenones.

**Nerve Growth Factor (NGF)** is a protein that:
- Promotes growth and survival of neurons
- Supports myelination (the insulating sheath around nerves)
- Facilitates neuroplasticity (the brain's ability to form new connections)
- Plays a role in memory consolidation

This is genuinely unique. No other legal supplement has been shown to reliably increase NGF in humans.

sections.heading: Evidence-Based Benefits
sections.body:
**Cognitive function:** A 2009 Japanese study on older adults with mild cognitive impairment found significant improvements in cognitive function scores after 16 weeks of lion's mane supplementation. Scores declined when supplementation stopped.

**Depression and anxiety:** A 2010 study on menopausal women found lion's mane reduced anxiety and depression scores. The mechanism may be related to NGF's role in hippocampal neuroplasticity.

**Nerve regeneration:** Animal studies show accelerated nerve repair after injury. Human evidence is limited but a small study on peripheral nerve injuries showed benefit.

**Gut health:** Lion's mane has prebiotic properties and may protect against gastric ulcers. It also contains beta-glucans that support immune function.

**Important caveat:** Most robust evidence is from animal studies or small human trials. Lion's mane is promising but not as well-studied as mainstream supplements like omega-3 or vitamin D.

sections.heading: Dosing and What to Buy
sections.body:
**Dose:** 500-3,000mg daily. Most studies use 750-1,000mg.

**What to look for:**
- **Fruiting body extract** -- Contains hericenones. Most researched.
- **Dual extract (fruiting body + mycelium)** -- Contains both hericenones and erinacines. Theoretically best but more expensive.
- **Avoid mycelium-on-grain products** -- Many cheap lion's mane supplements grow mycelium on grain substrate, then grind the whole thing. You end up with mostly starch filler and minimal active compounds.
- **Beta-glucan content** -- Quality extracts list beta-glucan percentage (aim for >25%).
- **Hot water or dual extraction** -- Required to make the active compounds bioavailable.

**Good brands:** Real Mushrooms, Nootropics Depot, Host Defense (Paul Stamets' brand, though it's mycelium-based), Oriveda

**Timing:** Morning or early afternoon. Some people find it mildly stimulating.

sections.heading: Side Effects and Concerns
sections.body:
Lion's mane is generally very safe. Reported side effects are rare and mild:

- **Digestive discomfort** -- Some people experience mild bloating or stomach upset, especially at higher doses. Start at 500mg and increase gradually.
- **Skin itching** -- Occasionally reported. May be related to increased NGF affecting skin nerve sensitivity. Usually mild and temporary.
- **Allergies** -- If you're allergic to other mushrooms, use caution.

**Who should avoid it:**
- People with mushroom allergies
- People on blood thinners (lion's mane may have mild antiplatelet properties)
- People about to have surgery (stop 2 weeks before)

**Timeline:** Subtle cognitive effects may be noticeable within 2-4 weeks. Full neuroplasticity benefits likely take 2-3 months of consistent use.

Lion's mane pairs well with [Alpha-GPC](/blog/best-supplement-stack-for-focus) for a comprehensive nootropic stack. Check interactions with the [Supplement Checker](/).

title: Should You Take Supplements Every Day or Cycle Them?
metaDescription: Some supplements work best daily, others should be cycled. Here's which supplements to take continuously and which need breaks.
intro: Not all supplements follow the same rules. Some need daily consistency to work, others lose effectiveness without breaks. Here's the definitive guide.

sections.heading: Take Daily (No Cycling Needed)
sections.body:
These work through consistent blood levels or saturation and should be taken every day:

- **Vitamin D** -- Fat-soluble, builds up slowly. Daily dosing maintains stable blood levels.
- **Omega-3** -- Anti-inflammatory benefits require consistent intake. Skipping days reduces tissue omega-3 levels.
- **Magnesium** -- Your body uses magnesium daily for 300+ enzymatic reactions. Daily replenishment is necessary.
- **Creatine** -- Works through muscle saturation. Skipping days depletes stores by ~1.7%/day.
- **Probiotics** -- Most strains don't colonize permanently. Daily intake maintains the population.
- **Collagen** -- Building amino acid pools requires consistent intake.
- **Vitamin K2** -- Works in concert with D3. Take daily with your D3.
- **Zinc** -- Your body has no zinc storage mechanism. Daily intake is needed.

These are also safe for indefinite long-term use at recommended doses.

sections.heading: Cycle Recommended
sections.body:
These may lose effectiveness or carry risks with continuous use:

- **Ashwagandha** -- 8 weeks on, 2-4 weeks off. Thyroid effects and potential emotional blunting with continuous use. See our [cycling guide](/blog/do-i-need-to-cycle-ashwagandha).
- **Rhodiola** -- Best used situationally during high-stress periods, not continuously. 4-6 weeks on, 2 weeks off.
- **Melatonin** -- Works best as a short-term or situational tool, not a nightly crutch. See our [melatonin tolerance guide](/blog/does-melatonin-stop-working).
- **Caffeine** -- Tolerance builds within 1-2 weeks. Periodic breaks (1-2 weeks off every 2-3 months) reset sensitivity.
- **Pre-workout blends** -- Caffeine tolerance plus potential adrenal fatigue with continuous stimulant use.
- **Elderberry** -- Use during cold/flu season or at illness onset, not year-round. Some concern about immune overstimulation with continuous use.

sections.heading: Depends on Your Situation
sections.body:
**NAC** -- Can be taken daily long-term for glutathione support. Some practitioners recommend cycling (3 months on, 1 month off) but evidence for this is limited.

**Alpha-GPC / Choline** -- Daily use is fine for most people. Some nootropic users cycle 5 days on, 2 days off to maintain sensitivity.

**Lion's Mane** -- Can be taken daily. Some users cycle (4 weeks on, 1 week off) but no evidence this is necessary. The cognitive benefits seem to depend on consistent use.

**Iron** -- Take daily until deficiency is corrected, then stop or reduce to maintenance. Don't take iron indefinitely without monitoring levels -- excess iron is toxic.

**B vitamins** -- Daily is fine if deficient. If you're not deficient and just taking them "for energy," they're probably unnecessary to begin with.

sections.heading: The Simple Rule
sections.body:
**Nutrients (vitamins, minerals, omega-3, collagen):** Take daily. Your body needs these consistently.

**Adaptogens and herbs (ashwagandha, rhodiola, elderberry):** Cycle. These work on signaling systems that can adapt.

**Stimulants (caffeine):** Cycle or manage tolerance.

**Hormones (melatonin, DHEA):** Use situationally, not daily.

**Amino acids (creatine, NAC, glycine):** Daily is generally fine.

When in doubt, the supplement's mechanism tells you: if it corrects a deficiency or maintains a pool, take it daily. If it modulates a receptor or signaling pathway, cycling may preserve its effectiveness.

title: Best Supplements for PCOS (Polycystic Ovary Syndrome)
metaDescription: Inositol, berberine, vitamin D, and other evidence-based supplements for managing PCOS symptoms like irregular periods, insulin resistance, and acne.
intro: PCOS affects 1 in 10 women of reproductive age. While there's no supplement cure, several have strong clinical evidence for managing the underlying insulin resistance, hormonal imbalance, and inflammation that drive PCOS symptoms.

sections.heading: Tier 1: Strong Evidence
sections.body:
**Myo-Inositol + D-Chiro-Inositol (4,000mg + 100mg daily)**
This is the most important PCOS supplement. Inositol is an insulin sensitizer that's been compared to metformin in clinical trials. A 40:1 ratio of myo-inositol to D-chiro-inositol mimics the body's natural ratio and has the best evidence for:
- Restoring ovulation and menstrual regularity
- Reducing testosterone levels
- Improving egg quality for fertility
- Reducing insulin resistance

Multiple meta-analyses confirm its effectiveness. It's considered first-line nutritional therapy for PCOS by many reproductive endocrinologists.

**Vitamin D (2,000-4,000 IU)**
67-85% of women with PCOS are vitamin D deficient. Correcting deficiency has been shown to improve insulin sensitivity, reduce androgens, and support ovulation. Always pair with K2.

sections.heading: Tier 2: Good Evidence
sections.body:
**Berberine (500mg 2-3x daily)**
An insulin sensitizer comparable to metformin in several PCOS studies. Reduces fasting glucose, insulin levels, and testosterone. Also improves lipid profiles. **Important:** If you're already on metformin, don't add berberine without medical supervision -- combining them can cause hypoglycemia.

**Omega-3 (2-3g EPA+DHA)**
Reduces inflammation and improves lipid profiles in PCOS. Some studies show reduced testosterone levels. Anti-inflammatory effects help with PCOS-related acne and mood issues.

**NAC (600mg 2x daily)**
Improves insulin sensitivity and has been shown to restore ovulation in some PCOS patients. A study found NAC was as effective as metformin for improving insulin sensitivity in PCOS. Also supports glutathione production which combats the oxidative stress common in PCOS.

sections.heading: Tier 3: Supportive
sections.body:
- **Magnesium (300-400mg)** -- Women with PCOS have higher rates of magnesium deficiency. Correcting it improves insulin sensitivity and sleep.
- **Zinc (30mg)** -- Reduces androgens and improves acne. Studies show zinc supplementation reduces hirsutism (excess hair growth) in PCOS.
- **Chromium (200-1000mcg)** -- Insulin sensitizer. Some studies show modest benefit for glucose metabolism in PCOS.
- **Spearmint tea (2 cups daily)** -- Anti-androgenic. Studies show it reduces free testosterone and improves hirsutism. Not a supplement per se, but worth adding to your routine.
- **Folate (methylfolate, 400-800mcg)** -- Important if trying to conceive. MTHFR mutations are more common in PCOS.

sections.heading: PCOS Stack
sections.body:
**Core (start here):**
- Myo-inositol 4,000mg + D-chiro-inositol 100mg (split AM/PM)
- Vitamin D3 2,000-4,000 IU + K2 (morning with food)
- Omega-3 2g EPA+DHA (with food)

**Add based on symptoms:**
- Insulin resistance: Berberine 500mg 2-3x daily (or NAC if on metformin)
- Acne/hirsutism: Zinc 30mg + spearmint tea
- Fertility: Folate + CoQ10 200mg
- Sleep/stress: Magnesium glycinate 400mg (evening)

**Timeline:** Inositol typically takes 3-6 months to restore regular cycles. Berberine and NAC show metabolic improvements within 8-12 weeks.

**Critical note:** These supplements complement, not replace, medical treatment. Work with your OB-GYN or endocrinologist.

title: Supplements That May Help With ADHD Symptoms
metaDescription: Omega-3, magnesium, iron, and zinc have evidence for improving ADHD symptoms. Here's what the research says and what's just hype.
intro: Supplements are NOT a replacement for ADHD medication for those who need it. But research shows certain nutrients play a role in attention, executive function, and dopamine production. Addressing deficiencies can meaningfully improve symptoms alongside standard treatment.

sections.heading: Nutrients Linked to ADHD
sections.body:
Multiple studies have found that people with ADHD are more likely to be deficient in specific nutrients:

- **Iron/Ferritin** -- Low ferritin is found in up to 84% of ADHD children in some studies. Iron is required for dopamine synthesis. Optimal ferritin for ADHD is above 50 ng/mL.
- **Zinc** -- Involved in dopamine metabolism and melatonin production. Deficiency is more common in ADHD populations.
- **Magnesium** -- 72% of ADHD children were magnesium deficient in one study. Magnesium supports neurotransmitter function and reduces hyperactivity.
- **Vitamin D** -- Deficiency correlates with ADHD severity in multiple studies.
- **Omega-3 DHA** -- ADHD is associated with lower blood levels of omega-3 fatty acids, particularly DHA.

The pattern: ADHD brains have higher nutrient demands, and deficiencies worsen symptoms.

sections.heading: Supplements With Clinical Evidence
sections.body:
**Omega-3 (EPA+DHA, 1-2g daily, EPA-dominant)**
The most-studied supplement for ADHD. A 2018 meta-analysis of 25 randomized controlled trials found omega-3 supplementation had a small but significant effect on improving attention and reducing hyperactivity. EPA appears more important than DHA for ADHD specifically. Higher doses (1.5-2g) tend to show better results.

**Iron (if ferritin is low)**
Supplementing iron in ADHD children with low ferritin has shown improvements in ADHD rating scales comparable to stimulant medication in some studies. **Only supplement if blood levels confirm deficiency** -- excess iron is toxic.

**Zinc (15-30mg)**
Several controlled trials show zinc supplementation improves attention and reduces hyperactivity, particularly in zinc-deficient individuals. One study found zinc enhanced the effectiveness of methylphenidate (Ritalin).

**Magnesium + Vitamin B6**
A combination of magnesium and B6 has shown improvements in hyperactivity, attention, and aggressiveness in ADHD children across multiple studies. Magnesium alone also helps with the sleep issues common in ADHD.

sections.heading: Promising But Less Evidence
sections.body:
- **Phosphatidylserine (200mg)** -- A phospholipid that supports cell membrane function. A few small studies show improvement in attention and impulsivity in ADHD children.
- **L-Theanine (200mg)** -- Improves alpha brain wave activity and promotes calm focus. May help with the restlessness component of ADHD without sedation.
- **Pycnogenol (pine bark extract, 1mg/kg)** -- A few controlled trials show reduced hyperactivity and improved attention. Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory mechanisms.
- **Vitamin D (if deficient)** -- Correcting deficiency has shown modest ADHD symptom improvement in some trials.

sections.heading: What Doesn't Work for ADHD
sections.body:
- **Sugar elimination** -- Despite the persistent myth, multiple controlled studies show sugar does not cause or worsen ADHD.
- **Megadose vitamins** -- High-dose B vitamins or vitamin C for ADHD have no evidence.
- **Ginkgo biloba** -- Occasionally marketed for focus, but no reliable evidence for ADHD.
- **Most nootropic stacks** -- Products marketed for "focus" and "brain optimization" are not studied for ADHD specifically.

sections.heading: ADHD Support Protocol
sections.body:
**Step 1: Test for deficiencies**
- Ferritin, zinc, magnesium, vitamin D, omega-3 index

**Step 2: Correct deficiencies**
- Iron bisglycinate (if ferritin < 50)
- Zinc 15-30mg
- Magnesium glycinate 200-400mg
- Vitamin D 2,000 IU

**Step 3: Add omega-3**
- EPA-dominant fish oil, 1.5-2g daily

**Step 4: Optional add-ons**
- L-Theanine 200mg (for calm focus)
- Phosphatidylserine 200mg (for attention)

**Important notes:**
- These supplements are adjuncts to standard ADHD treatment, not replacements
- Discuss with your prescribing doctor, especially regarding iron and zinc which can interact with stimulant medications
- Allow 8-12 weeks to evaluate effects

title: Fish Oil vs Krill Oil: Which Is Better?
metaDescription: Fish oil and krill oil both provide omega-3s but differ in absorption, cost, and side effects. Here's the honest comparison to help you choose.
intro: Krill oil marketing makes it sound like fish oil is obsolete. Fish oil advocates say krill is overpriced. The truth is somewhere in the middle.

sections.heading: The Key Difference: Phospholipids vs Triglycerides
sections.body:
**Fish oil** delivers omega-3s (EPA and DHA) in triglyceride form. This is how fats naturally occur in fish.

**Krill oil** delivers omega-3s bound to phospholipids. Phospholipids are more water-soluble, which theoretically improves absorption since they don't need as much bile acid to emulsify.

Studies comparing absorption show krill oil EPA and DHA reach blood levels slightly faster, but the total absorption over 24-72 hours is similar when doses are matched. The practical difference is small.

sections.heading: Head-to-Head Comparison
sections.body:
| Factor | Fish Oil | Krill Oil |
|---|---|---|
| EPA+DHA per capsule | 500-1000mg | 100-250mg |
| Cost per gram EPA+DHA | $0.03-0.08 | $0.15-0.30 |
| Absorption | Good | Slightly better |
| Fishy burps | Common (poor brands) | Rare |
| Astaxanthin | No | Yes (natural antioxidant) |
| Sustainability | Varies by brand | Generally well-managed |
| Capsule size | Large | Smaller |
| Oxidation risk | Higher | Lower (phospholipid-bound) |

sections.heading: When to Choose Fish Oil
sections.body:
**Pick fish oil if:**

- **You need high doses.** For therapeutic purposes (inflammation, joint pain, ADHD, depression), you need 2-4g EPA+DHA daily. Getting this from krill oil would require 8-16+ capsules and cost a fortune. Fish oil concentrates can deliver 1g per capsule.
- **You're on a budget.** Fish oil is 3-5x cheaper per gram of omega-3. For most people, this is the deciding factor.
- **You're already tolerating it well.** If your current fish oil doesn't cause burps or GI issues, there's no reason to switch.

**Best fish oil brands:** Nordic Naturals, Carlson, Sports Research, WHC. Look for IFOS certification and high EPA+DHA concentration.

sections.heading: When to Choose Krill Oil
sections.body:
**Pick krill oil if:**

- **Fish oil gives you terrible burps** even with quality brands and frozen capsules. Krill oil is much gentler on digestion.
- **You only need a maintenance dose.** If you eat fish regularly and just want a small daily omega-3 top-up (500mg EPA+DHA), krill oil works fine.
- **You want the astaxanthin bonus.** Krill oil naturally contains astaxanthin, a powerful antioxidant with skin and eye benefits.
- **You hate large capsules.** Krill oil capsules are noticeably smaller.

**The honest truth:** For most people at maintenance doses, krill oil and fish oil deliver similar results. The choice comes down to budget vs convenience. For therapeutic high-dose omega-3, fish oil is the only practical option.

Check omega-3 interactions with your stack at the [Supplement Checker](/).

title: Best Supplements for Menopause Symptoms
metaDescription: Evidence-based supplements for hot flashes, sleep disruption, mood changes, and bone loss during menopause. What actually works beyond HRT.
intro: Menopause brings a cascade of symptoms driven by declining estrogen. While hormone replacement therapy (HRT) is the most effective treatment, supplements can provide meaningful relief for women who can't or prefer not to use HRT.

sections.heading: For Hot Flashes
sections.body:
**Black Cohosh (20-40mg standardized extract)**
The most-studied herbal supplement for hot flashes. Multiple trials show it reduces hot flash frequency and severity. The German regulatory authority has approved it for menopausal symptoms. Takes 4-8 weeks for full effect. Avoid if you have liver conditions.

**Phytoestrogens / Soy Isoflavones (40-80mg)**
Soy isoflavones (genistein and daidzein) weakly bind to estrogen receptors. Meta-analyses show they reduce hot flash frequency by 20-25%. Work best in women who can convert daidzein to equol (about 30-50% of Western women have the right gut bacteria).

**Evening Primrose Oil (500-1000mg)**
Contains GLA (gamma-linolenic acid), an anti-inflammatory omega-6. Some small studies show modest hot flash reduction. Evidence is weaker than black cohosh but some women report significant benefit.

sections.heading: For Sleep and Mood
sections.body:
**Magnesium Glycinate (400mg)**
Addresses multiple menopause symptoms: sleep disruption, anxiety, muscle tension, and restless legs. Magnesium needs often increase during menopause. Glycinate form provides calming glycine as a bonus.

**Ashwagandha (300mg KSM-66)**
Reduces cortisol and anxiety. A 2021 study specifically on perimenopausal women found ashwagandha significantly improved quality of life, reduced hot flashes, and improved sleep compared to placebo.

**L-Theanine (200mg)**
Promotes calm without sedation. Helpful for the anxiety and irritability that often accompany hormonal shifts. Can be taken morning for daytime calm or evening for sleep.

**Valerian Root (300-600mg)**
Several studies on menopausal women show improved sleep quality. More evidence than most sleep herbs for this specific population. Take 30-60 minutes before bed.

sections.heading: For Bone Health
sections.body:
Estrogen decline accelerates bone loss. Women can lose up to 20% of bone density in the 5-7 years after menopause.

**Vitamin D3 (2,000-4,000 IU) + K2 (200mcg MK-7)**
Non-negotiable for menopausal bone health. D3 increases calcium absorption, K2 directs calcium to bones. Get blood levels tested -- many menopausal women need 4,000 IU.

**Calcium (500-600mg if dietary intake is low)**
Controversial. The latest evidence suggests dietary calcium is preferable to supplements, and high-dose calcium supplements may increase cardiovascular risk. If your diet includes dairy, leafy greens, and fortified foods, you may not need supplemental calcium. If supplementing, take no more than 500-600mg and always pair with D3+K2.

**Magnesium (300-400mg)**
60% of bone mineral content is magnesium. Deficiency accelerates bone loss. Most women don't get enough from diet alone.

sections.heading: Menopause Support Stack
sections.body:
**Morning:**
- Vitamin D3 2,000-4,000 IU + K2 200mcg (with food)
- Omega-3 2g (anti-inflammatory, cardiovascular, mood)
- Soy isoflavones 40-80mg (for hot flashes)

**Evening:**
- Magnesium glycinate 400mg
- Ashwagandha 300mg KSM-66 (with dinner)
- Black cohosh 20mg (for hot flashes)

**Before bed:**
- Valerian 300mg (if sleep is an issue)
- L-Theanine 200mg

**Important:** These supplements may interact with HRT, tamoxifen, and other medications. Phytoestrogens (soy, black cohosh) should be discussed with your oncologist if you have a history of hormone-sensitive cancer.

title: Creatine vs Pre-Workout: Do You Need Both?
metaDescription: Creatine and pre-workout supplements work differently. Here's whether you need both, just one, or can save your money entirely.
intro: Creatine and pre-workout are the two most popular gym supplements, but they work in completely different ways. Many people take both without understanding what each does. Here's the breakdown.

sections.heading: How They're Different
sections.body:
**Creatine** is a daily supplement that saturates your muscles over 2-4 weeks. It increases your phosphocreatine stores, allowing faster ATP regeneration during high-intensity efforts. You don't "feel" it -- it just quietly makes you 5-10% stronger and more powerful. Timing doesn't matter.

**Pre-workout** is an acute stimulant you take 30 minutes before training. The primary active ingredient is caffeine (150-400mg). You feel it immediately: increased alertness, energy, and reduced perceived effort. Some pre-workouts also contain citrulline, beta-alanine, and other performance ingredients.

Crucially: **creatine is not a pre-workout ingredient** even though many pre-workouts contain it. The 2-3g of creatine in a pre-workout is usually underdosed (you need 5g) and taking it only on training days defeats the purpose of daily saturation.

sections.heading: Do You Need Both?
sections.body:
**Creatine: Almost everyone benefits.** It's the most researched supplement in sports nutrition. Safe, cheap (~$0.20/day), and effective for strength, power, and even cognitive performance. There's almost no reason NOT to take it.

**Pre-workout: Depends on your needs.**
- If you train in the morning and need a boost: yes, it helps
- If you're already caffeinated and energized: probably not
- If you train in the evening: caffeine may wreck your sleep

**The honest assessment:** Creatine is a near-universal recommendation. Pre-workout is optional and situation-dependent.

sections.heading: Build Your Own vs Buy a Blend
sections.body:
Most pre-workout blends are overpriced and underdosed. Here's what a typical pre-workout contains and what you'd pay buying ingredients separately:

| Ingredient | Effective Dose | In Most Pre-Workouts | Cost (Separate) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Caffeine | 200-400mg | 150-300mg | $0.05 |
| Citrulline | 6-8g | 3-4g (underdosed) | $0.30 |
| Beta-Alanine | 3-5g | 1.6-2g (underdosed) | $0.15 |
| Creatine | 5g | 2-3g (underdosed) | $0.20 |
| L-Theanine | 200mg | Usually absent | $0.10 |

**DIY pre-workout cost: ~$0.80/serving**
**Commercial pre-workout: $1.50-2.50/serving**

You get proper doses, no artificial sweeteners or colors, and it costs less. The only downside: you have to measure powders instead of scooping one product.

sections.heading: The Optimal Approach
sections.body:
**Daily (regardless of training):**
- Creatine 5g (in water, coffee, or a shake)

**Pre-workout (training days only, 30 min before):**
- Caffeine 200mg (pill or coffee)
- L-Theanine 200mg (smooths out jitters)
- Citrulline 6-8g (pumps and endurance)
- Beta-alanine 3g (buffering for high-rep work)

**Skip if training after 4pm** -- the caffeine will hurt your sleep, which hurts your recovery more than the pre-workout helps your session.

See our full [gym stack guide](/blog/best-supplement-stack-for-gym) and check interactions with the [Supplement Checker](/).

title: Supplements That Interact With Birth Control Pills
metaDescription: Some supplements can reduce birth control effectiveness. St. John's Wort is the biggest risk, but others may matter too. Here's the complete list.
intro: If you're on hormonal birth control, some supplements can reduce its effectiveness or cause side effects. Most are safe, but a few are genuinely risky. Here's what you need to know.

sections.heading: High Risk: Avoid These
sections.body:
**St. John's Wort -- THE supplement to avoid.**
St. John's Wort induces CYP3A4 liver enzymes that metabolize the hormones in birth control pills. This speeds up hormone breakdown, reducing blood levels and potentially causing breakthrough bleeding or contraceptive failure.

This isn't theoretical -- the FDA, the WHO, and the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists all warn against combining St. John's Wort with hormonal contraceptives. Multiple case reports document unintended pregnancies.

**Activated charcoal**
If taken within 2-3 hours of your birth control pill, activated charcoal can bind to and absorb the hormones before your body does. "Detox" charcoal drinks and supplements are particularly risky if timed near your pill.

sections.heading: Moderate Concern: Use With Awareness
sections.body:
**Soy isoflavones / Phytoestrogens**
Soy isoflavones weakly bind to estrogen receptors. In theory, high-dose phytoestrogen supplements could compete with synthetic estrogen in birth control. The practical risk is likely low at normal dietary soy intake, but concentrated isoflavone supplements (40-80mg) warrant awareness. No documented contraceptive failures, but the interaction is biologically plausible.

**High-dose Vitamin C (over 1,000mg)**
Vitamin C can increase estrogen levels by inhibiting its breakdown. This isn't dangerous, but it could amplify birth control side effects (nausea, breast tenderness). If you take high-dose C, be aware of increased estrogen exposure.

**Saw Palmetto**
Anti-androgenic properties could theoretically interact with hormonal balance. Limited data, but worth mentioning to your doctor if you use both.

sections.heading: Safe With Birth Control
sections.body:
These supplements have no known interaction with hormonal contraceptives:

- **Vitamin D** -- Safe. Actually important because birth control can lower vitamin D levels.
- **Magnesium** -- Safe. Birth control depletes magnesium, so supplementing is a good idea.
- **Omega-3** -- Safe. No hormonal interaction.
- **Iron** -- Safe. Helpful since birth control can affect iron levels.
- **B vitamins** -- Safe and recommended. Oral contraceptives deplete B6, B12, and folate.
- **Probiotics** -- Safe. No interaction.
- **Zinc** -- Safe. Birth control may deplete zinc.
- **Creatine** -- Safe. No hormonal interaction.
- **Ashwagandha** -- Safe at normal doses. No documented contraceptive interaction.
- **NAC** -- Safe. No interaction.

sections.heading: Nutrients Birth Control Depletes
sections.body:
Oral contraceptives are known to deplete several nutrients:

- **Vitamin B6** -- Up to 20% lower levels. Deficiency can cause mood changes and fatigue.
- **Folate** -- Reduced absorption. Important to address before pregnancy.
- **Vitamin B12** -- Modestly lower levels.
- **Magnesium** -- Increased urinary excretion.
- **Zinc** -- Reduced blood levels.
- **Vitamin D** -- Some studies show lower levels.
- **Vitamin C** -- Oral contraceptives increase C utilization.
- **Selenium** -- Modestly depleted.

**If you're on birth control, consider supplementing:** B-complex, magnesium, zinc, and vitamin D. These address the most common depletions and support overall health.

title: Whey Protein vs Plant Protein: Which Is Better?
metaDescription: Whey protein has a better amino acid profile and absorption, but plant protein has closed the gap. Here's the honest comparison for muscle building.
intro: The protein powder debate has evolved. Whey used to be the clear winner, but modern plant protein blends have closed the gap significantly. Here's where things actually stand.

sections.heading: Why Whey Has Been King
sections.body:
Whey protein's advantages are real:

- **Complete amino acid profile** -- Contains all 9 essential amino acids in optimal ratios for muscle protein synthesis
- **High leucine content (~11%)** -- Leucine is the primary trigger for muscle protein synthesis. Whey has more than any plant source.
- **Fast absorption** -- Whey is rapidly digested and amino acids peak in blood within 60-90 minutes
- **Decades of research** -- More studied than any other protein source for muscle building

A 2019 meta-analysis confirmed that whey protein supplementation significantly increases muscle mass and strength when combined with resistance training.

sections.heading: Where Plant Protein Has Caught Up
sections.body:
Modern plant protein blends have addressed most of whey's historical advantages:

**Amino acid completeness:** Single-source plant proteins (pea, rice, soy) have incomplete profiles. But blends (pea + rice, for example) match whey's amino acid profile almost exactly. Look for blended formulas.

**Leucine gap:** Plant proteins have less leucine per serving, but this is solved by either:
- Taking a slightly higher dose (30-40g instead of 25g)
- Choosing soy protein (which has a leucine content closer to whey)
- Adding a leucine supplement (3g)

**Muscle building results:** A 2021 study found that pea protein produced equivalent muscle gains to whey protein over 12 weeks of resistance training when matched for protein dose. Similar results have been found with soy protein.

sections.heading: Head-to-Head Comparison
sections.body:
| Factor | Whey | Plant Blend |
|---|---|---|
| Muscle building | Excellent | Very good (nearly equal) |
| Leucine per serving | ~2.7g per 25g | ~1.8g per 25g |
| Digestibility (PDCAAS) | 1.0 (perfect) | 0.9-1.0 (blends) |
| Taste | Good | Improved but still grainier |
| Mixability | Smooth | Can be chalky |
| Acne risk | Higher (insulin/IGF-1) | Lower |
| Bloating/gas | Common (lactose) | Less common (but some get gas from pea) |
| Environmental impact | Higher | Lower |
| Cost per serving | $0.80-1.50 | $1.00-2.00 |
| Allergen risk | Dairy | Soy, pea (lower overall) |

sections.heading: Which Should You Choose
sections.body:
**Choose whey if:**
- You tolerate dairy well (no bloating, no acne)
- You want the most researched option
- You prefer the taste and texture
- Budget is a priority (slightly cheaper)

**Choose plant protein if:**
- Whey causes [acne](/blog/can-supplements-cause-acne), bloating, or digestive issues
- You're vegan or lactose intolerant
- Environmental impact matters to you
- You prefer a pea+rice or soy blend

**The practical truth:** If you eat enough total protein (1.6-2.2g/kg) from quality sources, the protein powder type makes a marginal difference. Pick the one you'll actually use consistently.

**Pro tip:** If you switch from whey to plant and notice less muscle growth, increase your serving size by 20% to compensate for the slight leucine and digestibility gap.

Check how protein supplements interact with your stack at the [Supplement Checker](/).

title: Best Supplements for Gut Health and Microbiome
metaDescription: Probiotics, prebiotics, L-glutamine, and other supplements that support gut health. What actually works for leaky gut, IBS, and microbiome balance.
intro: Your gut microbiome affects immunity, mood, skin, weight, and brain function. 'Gut health' supplements are a huge market, but most products are generic blends with no evidence. Here's what actually works.

sections.heading: Probiotics: Strain Specificity Matters
sections.body:
"Take a probiotic" is useless advice without specifying the strain. Different strains do completely different things:

**For IBS:** Bifidobacterium infantis 35624 (Align brand) -- The most studied probiotic for IBS. Shown to reduce bloating, pain, and irregular bowel movements.

**For antibiotic recovery:** Saccharomyces boulardii -- A yeast-based probiotic that's resistant to antibiotics. Prevents antibiotic-associated diarrhea and C. diff infection.

**For general gut health:** Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG -- The most studied probiotic strain overall. Supports immune function and gut barrier integrity.

**For mood (gut-brain axis):** Lactobacillus helveticus + Bifidobacterium longum -- The "psychobiotic" combination shown to reduce anxiety and cortisol in controlled trials.

**Dose matters:** Look for 10-50 billion CFU for therapeutic purposes. The 1 billion CFU in your yogurt isn't therapeutic.

sections.heading: Prebiotics: Feeding the Good Bacteria
sections.body:
Prebiotics are fiber compounds that feed beneficial bacteria. They're arguably more important than probiotics for long-term gut health:

**Psyllium husk (5-10g daily)** -- The most evidence-backed prebiotic fiber for gut health. Feeds Bifidobacterium and produces short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) that nourish your gut lining. Also regulates bowel movements in both directions (helps constipation AND diarrhea).

**Partially hydrolyzed guar gum (PHGG, 5g)** -- A soluble fiber that increases Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus. Less bloating than other prebiotics. Well-studied for IBS.

**Inulin/FOS (5-10g)** -- Found in chicory root. Strongly prebiotic but causes significant gas and bloating in many people. Start very low (2g) and increase slowly.

**Resistant starch** -- Found in cooked-then-cooled potatoes, green bananas, and supplements. Feeds butyrate-producing bacteria. Butyrate is the primary fuel for colon cells.

sections.heading: Gut Lining Support
sections.body:
**L-Glutamine (5-10g daily)**
The primary fuel source for enterocytes (intestinal lining cells). Studies show it supports gut barrier integrity and reduces intestinal permeability ("leaky gut"). Most studied in post-surgical and critically ill patients, but also used for general gut repair.

**Zinc Carnosine (75mg twice daily)**
Specifically studied for gut lining health. Shown to reduce intestinal permeability and support stomach lining. Has been used in Japan for decades for gastric issues.

**Collagen Peptides (10-15g)**
Provides glycine and proline, amino acids needed for gut lining repair. Not as well-studied for gut health specifically, but the amino acid profile supports connective tissue repair throughout the body.

**Butyrate (300-600mg)**
A short-chain fatty acid that's the preferred fuel for colon cells. Normally produced by gut bacteria from fiber. Supplementing directly can support gut lining health, especially if fiber intake is low.

sections.heading: Gut Health Protocol
sections.body:
**Phase 1: Remove irritants (2-4 weeks)**
- Identify food sensitivities (elimination diet)
- Reduce processed food, excess sugar, and alcohol
- Address any infections or SIBO (with medical help)

**Phase 2: Repair and replenish (ongoing)**

Morning (empty stomach):
- Probiotic (strain-specific to your needs)
- L-Glutamine 5g in water

With meals:
- Psyllium husk 5g (start at 2g, increase gradually)
- Zinc carnosine 75mg
- Omega-3 2g (anti-inflammatory)

Anytime:
- Collagen peptides 10g
- Bone broth (natural source of glutamine and collagen)

**Timeline:** Gut repair is slow. Expect 4-8 weeks for noticeable improvement and 3-6 months for significant healing.

**Important:** Chronic gut issues (blood in stool, severe pain, unexplained weight loss) need medical evaluation, not just supplements.

Check your gut health stack for interactions with our [Supplement Checker](/).

title: Why You Should Take Iron With Vitamin C
metaDescription: Vitamin C increases iron absorption by up to 67%. Here's the science behind the pairing and the optimal way to take them together.
intro: If you take iron supplements, vitamin C is your best friend. This is one of the most well-established supplement pairings in nutrition science. Here's exactly why it works.

sections.heading: The Science
sections.body:
Iron comes in two dietary forms:

**Heme iron** (from animal sources) -- Well-absorbed regardless of other factors.

**Non-heme iron** (from plants and supplements) -- Poorly absorbed. This is the form in virtually all iron supplements (ferrous sulfate, ferrous gluconate, iron bisglycinate).

Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) dramatically improves non-heme iron absorption through two mechanisms:

**1. Reduction.** Iron in supplements is mostly ferric iron (Fe3+). Your intestines can only absorb ferrous iron (Fe2+). Vitamin C converts ferric to ferrous, making it absorbable.

**2. Chelation.** Vitamin C forms a soluble complex with iron in your stomach, preventing it from being bound by absorption inhibitors like phytates, tannins, and calcium.

Studies show vitamin C can increase iron absorption by **50-67%** when taken together.

sections.heading: How to Pair Them
sections.body:
**Dose:** 200mg of vitamin C is sufficient to maximize iron absorption. You don't need mega-doses.

**Timing:** Take vitamin C at the exact same time as your iron supplement. The interaction happens in your stomach -- they need to be there together.

**Food sources work too:** A glass of orange juice (about 120mg vitamin C) with your iron supplement is effective. Other high-C foods: bell peppers, strawberries, kiwi, broccoli.

**Morning, empty stomach:** Iron absorbs best on an empty stomach. Take both first thing in the morning, 30-60 minutes before breakfast.

**Simple protocol:**
- Wake up
- Take iron supplement + 200mg vitamin C with water
- Wait 30-60 minutes
- Have breakfast and coffee (coffee blocks iron -- that's why you wait)

sections.heading: What Blocks Iron Absorption
sections.body:
While adding vitamin C, also avoid these around your iron dose:

- **Coffee and tea** -- Tannins reduce absorption by up to 80%. Wait 1 hour.
- **Calcium** -- Directly competes with iron. Take at different meals.
- **Dairy** -- Calcium in milk blocks iron.
- **Zinc** -- Competes for absorption. Space by 2 hours.
- **Antacids and PPIs** -- Reduce stomach acid needed for iron absorption.
- **Whole grains and legumes** -- Phytates bind iron. Not an issue if taking iron on an empty stomach.

The combination of taking iron on an empty stomach WITH vitamin C AND avoiding inhibitors can multiply your effective iron absorption several times over.

This matters because more absorbed iron means less iron sitting in your gut causing [constipation](/blog/why-does-iron-make-me-constipated) and side effects.

Check iron interactions with your full stack at the [Supplement Checker](/).

title: The Ultimate Supplement Timing Cheat Sheet
metaDescription: A quick-reference guide for when to take every common supplement: morning vs night, with food vs empty stomach, what to space apart.
intro: Stop guessing when to take your supplements. This is the quick-reference guide based on absorption science and practical experience. Bookmark this page.

sections.heading: Morning, Empty Stomach
sections.body:
Take these 30-60 minutes before breakfast:

| Supplement | Why Empty Stomach |
|---|---|
| Iron | Food reduces absorption 50%+ |
| Probiotics | Lower stomach acid = better survival |
| NAC | Amino acid competition with food |
| L-Tyrosine | Amino acid competition |
| Collagen | Better peptide absorption |
| Thyroid meds | Food blocks absorption (wait 60 min) |

**Pair iron with 200mg vitamin C** for maximum absorption.

sections.heading: Morning, With Breakfast
sections.body:
Take these with your first meal (ideally containing some fat):

| Supplement | Why With Food |
|---|---|
| Vitamin D3 + K2 | Fat-soluble, 32-50% better absorption with fat |
| Omega-3 / Fish Oil | Fat-soluble, reduces burps |
| CoQ10 | Fat-soluble, 3x better with food |
| B-Complex | Energizing, take early. Reduces nausea. |
| Curcumin | Needs fat + piperine for absorption |
| Ashwagandha | Fat improves withanolide absorption |
| Vitamin E | Fat-soluble |
| Astaxanthin | Fat-soluble |

sections.heading: Anytime (Consistency > Timing)
sections.body:
These work based on saturation or have no timing preference:

| Supplement | Notes |
|---|---|
| Creatine | 5g daily, any time. With food or without. |
| Vitamin C | Water-soluble, absorbs anytime |
| Magnesium citrate/malate | Either way. Some prefer morning for energy. |
| L-Theanine | Morning with caffeine OR evening for calm |
| Quercetin | With food preferred but flexible |

sections.heading: Evening / Before Bed
sections.body:
Take these in the evening for sleep support or to avoid daytime drowsiness:

| Supplement | Why Evening |
|---|---|
| Magnesium glycinate | GABA activation, sleep support |
| Glycine (3g) | Lowers body temperature, deepens sleep |
| Melatonin (0.3-0.5mg) | 30 min before target sleep time |
| Apigenin (50mg) | Mild GABA agonist |
| Zinc | Mildly sedating, with dinner |
| Valerian | 30-60 min before bed |

sections.heading: Supplements to Space Apart
sections.body:
These compete for absorption and should be taken at least 2 hours apart:

| Don't Combine | Reason |
|---|---|
| Iron + Calcium | Calcium blocks iron absorption |
| Iron + Zinc | Compete for absorption |
| Iron + Coffee/Tea | Tannins block iron by 80% |
| Zinc + Copper | Compete (but small amounts of copper WITH zinc are fine) |
| Calcium + Magnesium | Compete at high doses |
| Thyroid meds + Iron/Calcium | Minerals bind to medication |

**The simplest approach:** Morning = energizing + fat-soluble supplements with breakfast. Evening = calming supplements before bed. Space iron away from everything else.

For a personalized timing schedule based on your specific supplements, use the [Supplement Checker](/) -- it generates morning and evening splits automatically.
